description: Goroutine-Native Microkernel — Go channels and select as OS IPC, microkernel service decomposition, syscall ABI, ELF loader

userStories.title: Page-frame free-list allocator
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need a page-frame allocator that can both allocate and free 4 KiB pages so that tasks, channels, and page tables can release memory when no longer needed.
acceptanceCriteria: Add freePage(paddr uintptr) to src/vm.go that pushes the page onto a singly-linked free list (first 8 bytes of the free page store the next pointer)
Modify allocPage() to pop from the free list first; only bump nextFreePage if the free list is empty
Add a freeListHead uintptr global variable initialized to 0
Existing callers of allocPage() in vm.go, scheduler.go, userspace.go, smp.go, gdt.go continue to work unchanged
Test in main.go: allocate a page, free it, allocate again — serial log shows same physical address returned
Kernel boots and all existing demos pass in QEMU (make run)
make build succeeds with no linker errors
userStories.notes: Foundation for all subsequent stories. Only modifies src/vm.go and adds a test in src/main.go.

userStories.title: WaitQueue primitive
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need a WaitQueue data structure so that tasks can block on a condition and be woken by another task or an interrupt handler.
acceptanceCriteria: Define WaitQueue struct in src/scheduler.go with a fixed-size array of waiting task IDs (max 16) and a count
Implement waitQueueSleep(wq *WaitQueue) that sets current task to taskBlocked, appends its ID to the queue, and calls schedule()
Implement waitQueueWakeOne(wq *WaitQueue) that dequeues the first task ID and sets it to taskReady
Implement waitQueueWakeAll(wq *WaitQueue) that sets all queued tasks to taskReady and resets count to 0
wakeOne and wakeAll are safe to call from interrupt context (no blocking, no allocation)
Kernel boots and all existing demos still work in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-001. WaitQueue is used by channels (US-005/006) and sleep queue (US-003).

userStories.title: yield() — voluntary CPU relinquish
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need a yield() function so that a task can voluntarily give up the CPU to the next ready task.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement yield() in src/scheduler.go that sets current task to taskReady and calls schedule()
schedule() still performs round-robin selection of the next ready task
yield() is callable from any kernel task
Test: one demo task calls yield() in its loop instead of spin-waiting; serial log shows it gets preempted and rescheduled
Kernel boots and all existing demos still work in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Simple addition to scheduler.go. Prerequisite for taskSleep.

userStories.title: taskSleep() — timed blocking
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need taskSleep(ticks) so that a task can block for a specified number of PIT ticks instead of spin-waiting.
acceptanceCriteria: Extend Task struct with wakeupTick uint64 field
Implement taskSleep(ticks uint64) that computes wakeupTick = pitTicks + ticks, sets task to taskBlocked, inserts into a sleep queue (fixed-size sorted array), and calls schedule()
Timer IRQ handler (handleTimer in pit.go or main.go) checks sleep queue head and wakes all tasks whose wakeupTick <= pitTicks before calling schedule()
Refactor all 3 demo tasks (demoTaskA/B/C) to use taskSleep() instead of the spin-wait pattern (for pitTicks < target { hlt() })
Preemptive round-robin still works for tasks that do not call taskSleep()
Serial log shows demo tasks sleeping and waking at correct intervals
Kernel boots and demo tasks switch correctly in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-002, US-003. After this, the scheduler supports blocking primitives needed by channels.

userStories.title: Channel struct and chanCreate
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need a Channel data structure with a bounded ring buffer so that channels can be created with a specified capacity.
acceptanceCriteria: Create new file src/channel.go
Define Channel struct: fixed-capacity ring buffer of uintptr slots (max 256), sender WaitQueue, receiver WaitQueue, readIdx/writeIdx/count/capacity fields
Implement chanCreate(capacity int) *Channel that allocates and returns an initialized channel; capacity=0 means unbuffered
Channel uses a statically allocated pool (e.g., [32]Channel array) to avoid heap allocation complexity
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-002. Sets up data structure; send/recv come in US-006.

userStories.title: chanSend and chanRecv — blocking channel operations
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need blocking send and receive operations on channels so that tasks can communicate via message passing.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement chanSend(ch *Channel, val uintptr) in src/channel.go: if buffer full, block sender on ch.senderWQ via waitQueueSleep; when space available, write val to ring buffer, increment count, wake one receiver
Implement chanRecv(ch *Channel) uintptr: if buffer empty, block receiver on ch.receiverWQ via waitQueueSleep; when data available, read from ring buffer, decrement count, wake one sender
Unbuffered channel (capacity=0): chanSend blocks until a receiver is waiting; chanRecv blocks until a sender is waiting; value is transferred directly (rendezvous)
Implement chanTrySend(ch *Channel, val uintptr) bool: non-blocking send, returns false if buffer full (for use from interrupt context)
Test in main.go: create a buffered channel (capacity 4), spawn producer task that sends 10 values, spawn consumer task that receives them; serial log shows correct order of values
Test: create unbuffered channel, two tasks rendezvous; serial confirms both proceed after exchange
Kernel boots and all demos work in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-005, US-004 (taskSleep for WaitQueue). Core IPC primitive.

userStories.title: selectWait — multiplexed channel wait
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need a selectWait function that blocks until any one of multiple channels is ready so that a service task can listen on multiple inputs.
acceptanceCriteria: Define SelectCase struct in src/channel.go: pointer to Channel, direction (send=0/recv=1), value (for send cases)

Implement selectWait(cases []SelectCase) (index int, val uintptr): check all cases for immediate readiness; if none ready, register task on all relevant wait-queues and block; on wakeup, find which case is ready, deregister from all other wait-queues, execute the operation, return index and value
If multiple cases are ready simultaneously, choose the first ready in array order
No spurious double-wake: task is removed from all wait-queues atomically upon wakeup
Test: a task selects on two channels; separate producer tasks send to each at different times; serial log shows correct dispatch to the right case index
Kernel boots and all demos work in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-006. Most complex concurrency primitive. Lock ordering: acquire in channel-address order to prevent deadlock.

userStories.title: Microkernel service — keyboard task
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I want the keyboard driver to run as an isolated kernel task that publishes KeyEvent messages on a channel.
acceptanceCriteria: Define KeyEvent struct in src/keyboard.go with scancode uint8 and ascii byte fields
Create keyboardChannel global channel (capacity 16) during keyboard init
Modify IRQ1 handler to use chanTrySend to publish KeyEvent to keyboardChannel (drop if full, never block in interrupt context)
Create keyboardConsumerTask() in src/keyboard.go: loops calling chanRecv(keyboardChannel), handles VGA echo buffer logic and serial logging
Spawn keyboardConsumerTask as a kernel task during init in main.go
Remove VGA echo logic from the IRQ handler — it now lives only in the consumer task
Keyboard still echoes characters to VGA correctly in QEMU
Serial logging of keystrokes still works
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-006. First microkernel service decomposition.

userStories.title: Microkernel service — serial output task
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I want serial output to be handled by a dedicated kernel task so that writes from multiple tasks are serialized without explicit locking.
acceptanceCriteria: Create serialChannel global channel (capacity 16) in src/serial.go
Create serialTaskEntry() in src/serial.go: loops calling chanRecv(serialChannel), writes received uintptr (cast to string pointer) to COM1 byte by byte
Implement serialSend(msg string) helper that sends to serialChannel via chanSend (blocking, for use from task context)
Direct serialPrint/serialPrintln remain available for use from interrupt context and early boot (before serialChannel exists)
Spawn serialTaskEntry as a kernel task during init in main.go
Boot log and demo output still appear on serial in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-006. Keep direct serial functions for interrupt/boot context.

userStories.title: Microkernel service — filesystem task
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I want the filesystem to run as a kernel task that receives FSRequest messages and replies on per-request channels.
acceptanceCriteria: Define FSRequest struct in src/fs.go: op uint8 (0=create, 1=write, 2=read, 3=list), name string, data []byte, replyCh *Channel
Define FSResponse struct: ok bool, data []byte, names []string
Create fsRequestChannel global channel (capacity 8)
Create fsTaskEntry() in src/fs.go: loops calling chanRecv(fsRequestChannel), dispatches op to existing fsCreate/fsWrite/fsRead/fsList, sends FSResponse on replyCh
Implement helper functions: fsSendCreate(name) bool, fsSendWrite(name, data) bool, fsSendRead(name) []byte, fsSendList() []string — each creates a reply channel, sends request, blocks on reply
Spawn fsTaskEntry as a kernel task during init in main.go
Refactor filesystem demo in main.go to use fsSendCreate/fsSendWrite/fsSendRead/fsSendList helpers
Serial log confirms create/write/read/list still work correctly
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-006. Existing fs functions become internal to the FS task.

userStories.title: Syscall dispatch table with register-based ABI
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need a proper syscall dispatch table with register-based argument passing so that userspace programs can invoke multiple kernel services through int 0x80.
acceptanceCriteria: Modify isr.S: vector 0x80 stub passes pointer to saved register frame (on stack) to Go dispatcher so arguments can be read from rdi/rsi/rdx/r10/r8/r9 and return value written to rax
Define SyscallFrame struct in src/userspace.go matching the register layout pushed by isr_common
Implement syscallDispatch(frame *SyscallFrame) that reads frame.RAX as syscall number and dispatches to handler functions
Implement syscall handlers: sys_yield (0), sys_exit (1), sys_print (5) — sys_print reads buffer address from frame.RDI and length from frame.RSI, writes to serial
sys_exit sets current task to a new taskExited state and calls schedule() (task is never rescheduled)
Invalid syscall numbers set frame.RAX = 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF (-1)
Modify user program to call sys_print with a buffer containing 'Hello from Ring 3!' — verify output on serial
Kernel boots and userspace executes correctly in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-003 (yield). Modifies isr.S (assembly) and src/userspace.go. sys_send/sys_recv (channel syscalls) deferred to after ELF loader.

userStories.title: ELF64 parser — read and validate ELF headers
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need an ELF64 parser that reads and validates ELF file headers and program headers from the in-memory filesystem.
acceptanceCriteria: Create new file src/elf.go
Define ELF64 header struct (e_ident, e_type, e_machine, e_entry, e_phoff, e_phentsize, e_phnum) matching the ELF64 binary layout
Define ELF64 program header struct (p_type, p_flags, p_offset, p_vaddr, p_paddr, p_filesz, p_memsz, p_align)
Implement elfParse(data []byte) that validates: ELF magic (0x7f ELF), ELFCLASS64, EM_X86_64, ET_EXEC; returns entry point and slice of PT_LOAD program headers
Return error (bool) if any validation fails
Test: create a byte array with a valid ELF64 header in main.go, call elfParse, verify entry point is read correctly; serial log shows parsed values
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-010 (FS task for file reading). Parser only, no loading/execution.

userStories.title: ELF64 loader — map PT_LOAD segments and launch in Ring 3
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need the ELF loader to map program segments into userspace memory and launch the binary in Ring 3.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement elfLoad(name string) bool in src/elf.go: reads file from filesystem via fsSendRead, calls elfParse, then for each PT_LOAD segment: allocates pages covering [p_vaddr, p_vaddr + p_memsz), maps with pagePresent|pageWrite|pageUser, copies p_filesz bytes, zeros remaining p_memsz - p_filesz bytes (BSS)
Allocate user stack (8 KiB, 2 pages) at 0x7FFF0000, map with user flags
Call jumpToRing3(e_entry, stackTop) to start execution in Ring 3
Replace the hardcoded 6-byte user program in setupUserspace() with elfLoad('user.elf')

Test: cross-compile a minimal TinyGo program (or hand-craft a minimal ELF binary) that calls sys_print via int 0x80, store it in the filesystem, load and execute; serial shows output from user program
Kernel boots and user program executes correctly in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-012 (ELF parser), US-011 (syscall ABI for sys_print). Final milestone for userspace ELF execution.

userStories.title: Channel syscalls — sys_send and sys_recv from userspace
userStories.description: As a kernel developer, I need sys_send and sys_recv syscalls so that userspace programs can communicate with kernel services via channels.
acceptanceCriteria: Implement a channel ID table in src/channel.go: chanRegister(ch *Channel) uint64 returns an integer ID; chanLookup(id uint64) *Channel returns the channel pointer

Add sys_send (2) syscall handler: reads channel ID from frame.RDI, pointer from frame.RSI, length from frame.RDX; looks up channel, calls chanSend with the value; sets frame.RAX = 0 on success, -1 on invalid channel

Add sys_recv (3) syscall handler: reads channel ID from frame.RDI, buffer address from frame.RSI, max length from frame.RDX; looks up channel, calls chanRecv; copies received value to user buffer; sets frame.RAX = bytes written
Add sys_spawn (4) syscall handler: reads entry address from frame.RDI, creates a new task via createTask; sets frame.RAX = task ID
Register keyboard and filesystem channels with IDs accessible from userspace
Test: ELF user program calls sys_recv on keyboard channel ID, then sys_send to print channel; serial shows round-trip
Kernel boots and user program communicates via channels in QEMU
make build succeeds
userStories.notes: Depends on US-013 (ELF loader), US-006 (channels). Completes the userspace channel-based IPC story.
